title: Relevance vs. redundancy; the curse of dimensionality
key_concepts: Feature selection is not just about shrinking data size; it improves model interpretability, reduces training time, and mitigates the curse of dimensionality.
A feature can be relevant to the target but redundant if another feature provides the exact same information.

The 'curse of dimensionality': as feature count grows, the volume of the feature space increases exponentially. Data becomes sparse, and distance metrics become less meaningful, which severely degrades distance-based models.

definitions.Relevance: The degree to which a feature contains useful information about the target variable.
definitions.Redundancy: The degree to which a feature duplicates information already captured by other features.

examples: Describe a scenario with a relevant but redundant feature: predicting house prices using both 'square meters' and 'square feet'. Both are highly relevant to the price, but keeping both is redundant.
relations: Sets up the fundamental motivation for the algorithmic methods in s02-s04.
Builds on Ch 3 model sensitivity: distance-based models are particularly vulnerable to the curse of dimensionality.

figures.caption: Ilustrasi kutukan dimensi (curse of dimensionality) pada ruang satu, dua, dan tiga dimensi.
figures.description: Show how data points become increasingly sparse as the number of dimensions increases from a line, to a square, to a cube.

exclude: Deep mathematical proofs of distance metric degradation in high dimensions.
